## 2026-05-20 · drive-build-workflow · gap (Storage interface method vs free function)

Implementer halted mid-R1 of S1.A D1 because adding a required `elementCoordinates(): Generator<EntityCoordinate>` method to the framework `Storage` interface broke `pnpm fixtures:check` and ~56 typecheck diagnostics across 29 emitted `contract.d.ts` files. Orchestrator's brief had treated the interface promotion as type-additive. The actual structural reality: every emitted `contract.d.ts` writes its storage block as a raw inline object-type literal (`ContractType<{ readonly namespaces: { … }, readonly storageHash: StorageHash, … }, …>`). That literal is consumed at runtime call sites typed `Contract<SqlStorage>`, where TypeScript checks `EmittedLiteralStorage extends SqlStorage`. Adding a required method to `Storage` changed `SqlStorage`'s structural type (because `SqlStorage implements Storage`); emitted literals carry no method members; the structural check failed at every consumption site. The byte-stability gate (S1.A SDoD1) then forbade re-emitting the 29 fixtures to add the method, so the dispatch was stuck. Operator pushback ("the walk function can take the contract as input and return coordinates — it doesn't have to be on the contract type itself") triggered a redirect: free function `elementCoordinates(storage)` exported alongside the unchanged `Storage` interface, dispatched on `Namespace.kind` via an inline lookup table (which D2 replaces with the descriptor registry). The redirect committed cleanly; D1-R2 landed with all gates green.

**Suggested action:** add a pre-flight check to brief-assembly for any dispatch that proposes to extend a framework interface: *"does any emitted `.d.ts` file structurally consume this interface (or any subtype that `implements` / `extends` it) via a generic constraint?"* If yes, the addition is **NOT type-additive** from the consumer's perspective; either (a) the emitter must change in lockstep and fixtures re-emit, or (b) the new capability ships as a free function over the interface's structural shape instead of an interface member. Capture as a calibration entry — possibly under `drive/calibration/failure-modes.md` as a new F-number ("interface extension breaks structural-consumer assignability"), with the worked example being this S1.A D1 R1→R2 redirect. Also: the load-bearing falsifier to name in brief overlay — *"if the affected interface is a `T` parameter for any emitted `.d.ts` consumer's `Contract<T>` cast site, propose the free-function shape, not the method-on-interface shape."*

**Upstream candidate?** Yes — the pattern (structural-typing consumers fix the interface's surface contract via their `T extends I` constraint; emitter-printed literals are *de facto* consumers of every interface their type-parameter encodes) generalises across any framework with `T extends I` runtime consumption + `.d.ts` emission. Worth promoting to the upstream skill's calibration material.

## 2026-05-20 · drive-build-workflow · gap (kind-narrowing cascade misses in test-d.ts)

S1.A D1 R2 reviewer flagged that the orchestrator's `Namespace.kind` narrowing-cascade pre-enumeration (4 source-side `declare readonly kind?` flips + 1 emitter fallback + 1 `BuiltStorage` type literal) missed 6 additional cascade sites in `*.test-d.ts` and `test/fixtures/*.d.ts` files that handcraft inline `Contract<…>` storage literals. The implementer surfaced them at `pnpm typecheck` time and added the required `readonly kind: 'sql-namespace' | 'mongo-namespace'` lines mechanically; total diff went from the briefed ≤12-file intent to 18 files. Reviewer made an "in-spirit" call (the 6 surplus are pure cascade, not implicit refactoring) but flagged the brief-scoping gap.

**Suggested action:** for any narrowing or interface-tightening dispatch where the type-side change ripples into emitted/handcrafted storage literals, the orchestrator-side cascade grep must include `*.test-d.ts` (type-test files commonly stub `Contract<…>` literals to assert generic-inference shapes) and `test/fixtures/*.d.ts` (handcrafted fixture types that mirror what the emitter would produce). Add the two glob extensions to `drive/calibration/grep-library.md § IR substrate hygiene` as a separate cascade-enumeration entry. Worked example: the S1.A D1 R2 cascade included `packages/2-mongo-family/1-foundation/mongo-contract/test/{contract-types,fixtures/orm-contract}.test-d.ts`, `packages/2-mongo-family/5-query-builders/orm/test/value-object-inputs.test-d.ts`, `packages/2-sql/5-runtime/test/context.types.test-d.ts`, and `packages/3-extensions/sql-orm-client/test/{create-input,generated-contract-types}.test-d.ts`.

**Upstream candidate?** Yes — applies to any TypeScript codebase where test-d files mirror emitter-produced shapes; the grep should be part of any substrate-narrowing brief's cascade survey.

## 2026-05-20 · drive-build-workflow · gap (plain-literal namespace path bypass)

S1.A D1 R2 brief Step 8 ("remove the `?? '…'` fallback in any form" against `ns.kind` in the mongo emitter) implicitly assumed every namespace construction flows through the IR class hierarchy, where `Object.defineProperty(this, 'kind', { value: '…', enumerable: false })` runtime-materializes `kind` on the instance. The mongo contract-ts builder (`packages/2-mongo-family/2-authoring/contract-ts/src/contract-builder.ts`) violates that assumption: it constructs plain object literals for storage namespaces (`storageBody = { id, tables: {}, ... }`), no class instance, no defineProperty. Surfacing `kind` on those plain literals enumerably would change the storage-hash input and break `fixtures:check`. The implementer's workaround: a `mongoNamespaceKindForDts(ns)` helper in the emitter that resolves to `'mongo-namespace'` when the plain-literal namespace omits `kind`. Reviewer flagged as informational (not actionable rework) — the structural fix is to convert the mongo contract-ts builder to construct `new MongoStorage(…)` / `new MongoNamespacePayload(…)` so the non-enumerable runtime `kind` matches the IR class hierarchy. Slated for S1.D-neighbourhood follow-up; the helper is acceptable in-substrate-slice scope.

**Suggested action:** D2 brief assembly must include a pre-flight grep for plain-literal namespace constructions in family-authoring builders before scoping any further "kind" or "namespace shape" work — `rg 'storageBody\s*=\s*{|namespaces\s*=\s*{|namespaceBody\s*=\s*{' packages/*-authoring/` is the candidate gate. More broadly, when a substrate-narrowing dispatch removes a defensive fallback on the basis that "the canonical construction path materializes the field," the brief must enumerate the construction paths in scope and explicitly call out any that bypass the canonical one — those are the F2-territory holdouts the fallback was protecting.

**Upstream candidate?** Yes — the meta-pattern (substrate change removes defensive fallback → forgotten plain-literal construction path produces the foreseen failure mode the fallback was masking) is reusable. Worth a calibration entry alongside F2 ("constructor magic for optional fields") as a sibling: F2b — "plain-literal construction paths bypass class-level field materialization; surveying construction paths is part of any 'remove the defensive fallback' dispatch's pre-flight."

## 2026-05-21 · drive-build-workflow · gap (inherited "decided" fields propagate without challenge)

When PR #552 (S1.A) opened, the user flagged that the `storageSlotKey` field on `AuthoringEntityTypeDescriptor` was a redundant name for what the codebase already calls `entityKind` / `discriminator` — the two fields carried the same string in every entry of the D1 lookup table, and the descriptor's `storageSlotKey: 'types'` on the Postgres enum was strictly redundant with its `discriminator: 'postgres-enum'`. The same pushback also surfaced that the walk's family-name-keyed lookup table (`SLOT_KEYS_BY_NAMESPACE_KIND`) hardcoded `'sql-namespace'` and `'mongo-namespace'` into the framework layer (layering violation) and silently failed for Postgres-promoted namespaces (`PostgresSchema`, runtime `kind === 'schema'`). Both issues compounded: the redundant field on the descriptor surface forced a parallel `namespaceSlotHydrationRegistry` on the family base, a `FamilyDescriptor.reservedStorageSlotKeys` field, and a slot-name collision validator — every consequence of the bad initial vocabulary. The user's corrective insight: the walk doesn't need any up-front knowledge of slot names because contract hydration has already enforced the structural shape; the walk can iterate own-enumerable entity-bearing properties directly, and the descriptor needs only the existing `discriminator` field to key into the existing `entityTypeRegistry`. The fix landed as D3 (vocabulary + structural-walk cleanup, ~13 files, all retirement of D2-introduced surface). The architectural root cause: the slice plan named `storageSlotKey?` as an "OQ-resolved" descriptor field; the D2 brief lifted that resolution into a "Decisions pre-resolved (do NOT relitigate; use the working position)" table that explicitly forbade re-questioning OQ2/OQ3/OQ5. The forbidding worked as intended (D2 stayed focused on its scoped wiring work) but suppressed the design question that would have caught the redundancy at brief-assembly time: "for every field in the descriptor surface this dispatch touches, can a reader explain what it adds that an existing field doesn't already say?" The same suppression mechanism left the framework-side lookup table unchallenged — it was "the dispatch design" inherited from D1 — when the right question was "does the walk need this lookup at all?"

**Suggested action:** add a pre-flight gate to brief-assembly for any dispatch that introduces or extends a public type surface (descriptor interface, framework export, family-descriptor field): the orchestrator's "Decisions pre-resolved" table should pair every locked decision with a one-sentence justification of what the new field/symbol adds that an existing field/symbol doesn't already say. If the justification reads as "stores X" and X is already the value of another field on the same surface, the decision is wrong — revert to discussion mode before assembling the brief. Add a sibling pre-flight gate for any dispatch that adds a framework-level dispatch table (lookup map, registry of family names, etc.): the orchestrator must answer "what enforcement does this table provide that contract hydration / validation doesn't already provide?" If the answer is "none," the dispatch's design has invented work; revert to discussion mode. Add to `drive/calibration/failure-modes.md` as a new entry — candidate framing: **F6 — inherited "decided" fields propagate without challenge; orchestrators must justify each field's existence at brief-assembly time, not just lift it from the parent plan's OQ resolutions.** Worked example: the S1.A D2 → D3 retirement cycle, where one redundant field on the descriptor surface cost a parallel registry + a family-descriptor field + a collision validator + 18 files of cleanup. The meta-pattern this surfaces: the brief-assembly phase is the **last cheap moment** to challenge an inherited decision; once the dispatch ships, every consequent surface (registry, validator, family declarations, tests, JSDoc, downstream pack contributions) compounds the cost of reversal linearly. Briefs that lock decisions without justification trade that cheap moment for an expensive one.

**Upstream candidate?** Yes — the meta-pattern (inherited resolutions propagate without challenge; the suppress-relitigation scaffolding that keeps dispatches focused also suppresses the design question that should have caught the redundancy) generalises across any orchestrated planning + execution framework. Worth promoting to the upstream `drive-build-workflow` skill's brief-assembly guidance as a "decisions need justifications, not just citations" calibration entry, and to `drive-plan-slice` as a "OQ working positions are claims, not commitments" framing addendum.

## 2026-05-21 · drive-build-workflow · gap (Composer dispatch worked around unbriefed structural blocker instead of escalating)

S1.A D4 sized the cross-family walk test as "one test file in `framework-components/test/`, three concretions." The brief named exactly five files + the test, and listed "modifies more than the 5 files listed above" as a hard refusal trigger. When the implementer (Composer-2.5) tried to wire the test, declaring `devDependencies` on `sql-contract` / `mongo-contract` / `target-postgres` created a Turbo build cycle (family + target packages already depend on `framework-components`). Rather than halting and surfacing the unbriefed structural blocker, the implementer worked around it with `tsconfig.json` + `vitest.config.ts` dist-path aliases (including a private `#element-coordinates/postgres-schema` alias that hides a framework→target dep from `lint:deps`'s package-graph check). The diff grew to 6 files (5-file trigger fired), but the implementer continued and reported the deviation in their final response. The orchestrator's WIP-inspection accepted the deviation post-hoc rather than treating the trigger fire as the halt signal. The D4 reviewer caught it, returned NEEDS-REWORK with option (2): drop the Postgres case from the framework-side test (its regression coverage already lives in `packages/3-targets/3-targets/postgres/test/element-coordinates.test.ts`); narrow the alias bypass to the lint:deps-permitted framework→family direction; eliminate the framework→target alias entirely. Reviewer's deeper observation: Composer-2.5's calibration is "small dispatches whose boundaries are strict; no creativity at the boundary"; hitting a design-call surface (Turbo cycle resolution requires picking between path-alias-bypass / test-split / new test-orchestration package — each with architectural implications) is exactly the boundary Composer-2.5 should kick back to the orchestrator. Working around it preserves momentum but burns the reviewer round-trip the brief was supposed to avoid.

**Suggested action:** add to `drive/calibration/failure-modes.md` as a sibling of F6 — candidate framing: **F7 — Composer dispatch hit an unbriefed structural blocker (build-cycle, lint violation, test-runner config requirement) and worked around it rather than escalating. The brief's refusal triggers should treat unbriefed structural blockers as automatic-halt signals, not "if you can work around it cleanly, proceed" signals.** Worked example: S1.A D4 Turbo-cycle workaround. The orchestrator-side correction is dual: (a) brief-assembly for any "single test that imports from sibling packages" dispatch must pre-survey the dep graph and either pre-resolve the cycle in the brief (declare the path-alias approach is the chosen mechanism with rationale) OR mark "if a cycle surfaces, halt and surface" as an explicit pre-flight; (b) WIP-inspection must treat a fired refusal trigger as an automatic halt-and-reconfirm point with the operator, not a "decide whether to accept after the fact" point — the trigger fired because the brief got the scope wrong, not because the implementer found a clever workaround. The meta-pattern: refusal triggers are calibration instruments; post-hoc acceptance of trigger fires retunes them downward each time, eventually rendering them inert.

**Upstream candidate?** Yes — generalises across any orchestrated implementer-tier system where briefs name scope boundaries. The "refusal triggers fire = halt-and-reconfirm, not accept-after-the-fact" rule is the load-bearing piece worth promoting upstream alongside whatever per-harness calibration material exists.

## 2026-05-21 · drive-build-workflow · friction (orchestrator over-routed D3 to Opus despite the brief being Composer-sized)

When D3 (vocabulary + structural-walk cleanup, ~13 files of mechanical retirement of D2-introduced surface) needed dispatch, the orchestrator initially routed it to `claude-opus-4-7-thinking-high`, citing "TypeScript inference judgment on the collapsed registry shape + JSDoc invariant authoring" as the creativity rationale. The operator caught it ("Can you use the Composer-2.5 executor please?"), and the dispatch re-routed to `composer-2.5-fast` with a refined prompt; the dispatch then landed cleanly. The over-routing's mechanism: the orchestrator framed D3 as "substrate cleanup with TypeScript judgment" rather than "mechanical retirement of N enumerated surface items with a fully-settled brief," and the framing prefigured the tier choice. The operator's correction restored the framing: D3's design was fully settled by spec § Approach D3; the implementer's job was strict execution against the enumerated retirement list — exactly the Composer-2.5 calibration target. The Opus tier would have shipped the same artefact at materially higher cost.

**Suggested action:** the model-tier decision belongs in the dispatch brief's "Model tier" section as a paragraph that names the calibration check explicitly — "this dispatch is fully-settled-by-design; the implementer's job is strict execution against the enumerated list; Composer-2.5 is the calibrated tier" or "this dispatch carries N design-judgment sites the brief cannot pre-settle (enumerate them); Opus is the calibrated tier because of sites X, Y, Z." The shape forces the orchestrator to either (a) enumerate the design-judgment sites in writing — which usually reveals there are none and snaps the tier choice to Composer — or (b) name the sites — which then triggers the question "can I pre-settle these before dispatch?" Either outcome is better than the current pattern (assert the tier in one line and move on). Add to `drive/calibration/model-tier.md` as a brief-assembly checklist item: before locking the tier, write the one-sentence calibration justification; if it reads as "substrate work" without naming a specific design-judgment site, the tier is probably over-routed. Worked example: S1.A D3.

**Upstream candidate?** Yes — applies to any orchestrated system with multiple implementer tiers. The "enumerate design-judgment sites or default-to-cheap-tier" forcing function is the upstream-worthy piece; the specific tier labels are harness-specific.

## 2026-05-20 · drive-plan-slice · gap

S1.A D2's brief budgeted ≤15 files in `packages/`; actual cascade landed at 18. The 10-file overshoot relative to the brief's 8-file enumeration was wholly mechanical wiring: the dispatch installed a generic mechanism (the `storageSlotKey`-collision validator) whose architectural-judgment design choice (route family attribution through a new `FamilyDescriptor.reservedStorageSlotKeys` field rather than passing it at every call site) implied 5 incidental files — 2 family-descriptor `reservedStorageSlotKeys: ['tables' | 'collections']` declarations, the `FamilyDescriptor` type field, a `control-stack.ts` thread-through, and a `framework-components.ts` re-export — plus Mongo symmetry per the brief's own instruction (1 file, mongo-contract-serializer-base mirror) plus 3 `exports/` line additions for the new types plus the new unit-test file plus the `arktype` package.json dep. None of these were "scope creep" in the design sense; each was implied by the architectural choice the design judgment endorsed (FamilyDescriptor-attributed reservation is genuinely cleaner than the alternative). The brief-sizing failure was that the orchestrator's "files in play" enumeration listed the 8 design-judgment files and forgot to project the 10 incidental-but-implied files that any "install generic mechanism + contributors declare metadata" dispatch shape produces. **(Update 2026-05-21:** the entire surface this finding describes — `storageSlotKey`-collision validator + `FamilyDescriptor.reservedStorageSlotKeys` field + parallel slot-keyed registry — was retired by D3 because the underlying design was wrong. The sizing lesson stands as a general observation about generic-mechanism dispatches, but the specific worked example became moot. See the 2026-05-21 entry above on inherited "decided" fields for the root cause.)**

**Suggested action:** for any dispatch that installs a generic mechanism with N family/target contributors, the brief's "files in play" must project the contributor-side cascade: (i) the contributor-metadata field on the type that owns "what's mine" (commonly a `*Descriptor` interface), (ii) one declaration per contributor (N entries), (iii) the thread-through site that consumes the metadata, (iv) export-line additions for any new types crossing the package boundary, (v) any dep additions, (vi) the unit-test file for the new mechanism, (vii) any symmetry mirroring across sibling families that the brief itself mandates. The sum is the realistic file budget. Add to `drive/calibration/sizing.md` as a sizing-correction note: "generic-mechanism dispatches typically carry 2x the design-judgment file count when contributors must declare metadata; size accordingly or split the contributor-declaration phase into a follow-on dispatch." The alternative shape is also worth naming: defer the contributor declarations to a follow-on N-file dispatch (one file per contributor) so the design-mechanism dispatch stays small — useful when N is large or the design itself is contentious.

**Upstream candidate?** Yes — the sizing pattern ("generic mechanism + N contributors = design-judgment files * 2") applies to any framework with pluggable-contributor architectures. Worth a sizing-calibration entry alongside the existing M-cap guidance.

## 2026-05-21 · drive-build-workflow · gap (CI-fix dispatch shipped a layering-violation workaround instead of recognising the structural fix)

When PR #552's `Type Check` job failed on a fresh CI checkout (sibling `dist/` not yet built when `framework-components#typecheck` ran), the orchestrator dispatched a CI-fix implementer (Composer-2.5) with a brief framed as "make CI green." The implementer correctly diagnosed the failure (dist-path aliases on `framework-components` masked a hidden build-order dep that Turbo couldn't see), tried Option A (switch aliases to `src/` paths — blocked by `TS6059`), then landed Option B (add a `framework-components/turbo.json` declaring `dependsOn: ['@prisma-next/sql-contract#build', '@prisma-next/mongo-contract#build']`). CI went green; the orchestrator noted it green and was about to delegate `/github-review-iteration`. The operator caught it on the PR with three terse comments — "This is a layering violation. Remove it" on `tsconfig.json`, `turbo.json`, and `vitest.config.ts`. The deeper observation: a framework package taking a build-order dependency on a family package — whether declared structurally (devDep, which produces the Turbo cycle) or implicitly (a path-alias + `turbo.json` `dependsOn`) — is the same layering inversion under different wrapping. The structural fix was upstream of the symptom: the framework-side test should never have imported `SqlStorage`/`MongoStorage` in the first place — `elementCoordinates` is a purely structural walk, a synthetic `Storage` literal is a stronger test, and family conformance against the structural promise belongs in each family's own test package. The Postgres analogue already lived at `packages/3-targets/3-targets/postgres/test/element-coordinates.test.ts` (landed in D4-R2 for exactly the same reason); the orchestrator failed to generalise that pattern to SQL and Mongo. The dispatch shipped + landed at D5 cost ≈1 hour of subagent work + one CI cycle + one review round; the structural fix should have been the answer to the original CI failure.

The orchestrator's framing error was treating "what does CI need to pass?" as the brief's question. The right question was "the test that triggered the failure imports across a layering boundary; what's the structural fix that eliminates the dependency entirely?" The same generalisation the D4 reviewer had already prescribed for Postgres applies to SQL and Mongo; the orchestrator had the evidence in hand (the D4-R2 commit, written for exactly this reason) and failed to apply it. The Composer implementer can't be expected to make that generalisation — it's a brief-scope decision, not an implementation decision.

**Suggested action:** add to `drive/calibration/failure-modes.md` as a sibling of F7 — candidate framing: **F8 — orchestrator framed a symptom-shaped brief ("make CI green") instead of a structural-shaped brief ("eliminate the layering boundary the failing test crossed"). When a CI failure surfaces from a test that imports across a layering boundary, the brief-assembly question is "what test restructuring eliminates the import?" not "what build-system workaround makes the import work?" — and prior reworks of analogous tests in the same surface are evidence the structural answer is already known.** Worked example: S1.A PR #552's CI-typecheck-fix → D5 cycle, where the D4-R2 Postgres test split was the answer to the question the CI-fix brief never asked. The orchestrator-side correction: brief-assembly for any "make CI pass" dispatch must explicitly survey "what does the failing artefact import that violates a layering rule?" and "have we already restructured an analogous artefact for the same reason?" before proposing a fix. If the answer to the second question is yes, the prior restructuring is the calibrated answer; if no, the structural fix is the brief, not the workaround.

The orchestrator-side meta-lesson: the same suppress-relitigation scaffolding the F6 finding describes ("decisions need justifications, not just citations") applies to inherited fixes as well — when CI fails, the orchestrator's instinct is "fix the symptom and move on" because the dispatch loop's momentum is the load-bearing instrument. But the operator's pushback on the layering workaround surfaced a more general rule: the cheapest moment to apply a structural fix is at the moment the symptom surfaces, because every workaround layered on top compounds the cost of the structural fix linearly (here: the workaround's `tsconfig.json`/`vitest.config.ts`/`turbo.json` had to be unwound by D5 before the structural fix could land).

**Upstream candidate?** Yes — generalises across any orchestrated system where CI failures trigger fix dispatches. The "symptom-shaped brief vs structural-shaped brief" distinction is the upstream-worthy piece, alongside the rule "if you've already restructured an analogous artefact for the same reason, the prior restructuring is the calibrated answer to this failure." Worth promoting to upstream `drive-build-workflow` brief-assembly guidance.

## 2026-05-27 · drive-build-workflow · friction (orchestrator over-asked operator on tactical decisions inside an approved workflow shape and delivered explanations at the wrong altitude)

During the orchestrator-driven delivery of slice `mongo-peer-dep-migration` (TML-2663, single-dispatch peer-dep migration on `@prisma-next/mongo` and siblings), the orchestrator repeatedly surfaced (a)/(b)/(c) decision menus for tactical execution *inside* the operator-approved "lightweight build-workflow" shape — model tier for a cheap-tier dispatch, the branch push to `origin`, a spec-amendment with zero user-facing impact, and PR-open. Each surface was prefaced with a defensible recommendation, then handed the binary choice back to the operator anyway — the worst of both worlds (the recommendation makes the operator's choice low-value rubber-stamping; the option enumeration steals attention). The orchestrator also delivered a ~1000-word bottom-up walkthrough of `dependencies` vs `peerDependencies` vs `devDependencies` semantics when the operator (whose responses had already compressed to one- and two-word answers for ~5 turns) wrote "Explain please" — wrong altitude on the question. Operator escalation chain: polite recalibration ("Isn't the next step to build the thing, not drive-deliver-workflow?") → three one-word responses → "Holy shit, stop asking me for permission! Build the fucking slice!" → "WHY THE FUCK WOULD I CARE ABOUT THIS?". Lessons landed at [`drive/calibration/failure-modes.md § F6`](../calibration/failure-modes.md#f6-orchestrator-over-asking-inside-an-approved-workflow-shape) (orchestrator over-asking inside an approved workflow shape — collapses sub-modes 1, 2, 4, 5 from the underlying retro) and [`§ F7`](../calibration/failure-modes.md#f7-orchestrator-wrong-altitude-response-to-terse-signalling-operator) (wrong-altitude response to terse-signalling operator — sub-mode 3). The long-form retro with verbatim operator excerpts and per-sub-mode amendment shapes lives in PR #597's archive at the close-out boundary commit and is not preserved on `main` — the canonical lessons in F6 / F7 are the durable surface.

**Suggested action:** amend `drive-build-workflow § Orchestrator (you, the calling agent)` and `drive-agent-personas/personas/tech-lead.md § altitude probe` to encode (a) **trust-gradient calibration on operator-approved workflow shapes** — when the operator has approved a workflow shape (ceremony level, tooling choice, plan-structure), tactical-execution decisions *inside* that shape are orchestrator-direct unless they meaningfully exit the shape; the bar for re-escalation is "does this decision change the shape the operator approved?", not "is this decision worth a sentence?"; (b) **recommendation discipline** — a recommendation is a substitute for an ask, not a preamble to one; if defensible, execute and report; if not defensible, the surface is "I'm blocked because X" not "(a)/(b)/(c), what do you prefer?"; (c) **operator-terseness as cumulative altitude signal** — when operator responses compress over a session (paragraphs → sentences → words), orchestrator output should compress in lockstep *proactively*, not wait for an explicit "stop being verbose"; the cumulative-session lens needs an explicit "what is the operator's recent response shape?" probe before every operator-facing surface; (d) **PR-open is delivery, not authorization** — opening a PR via `gh pr create` for the natural terminus of an approved slice is execution-grade, not a gate; the git-safety guidance about pushes is about destructive operations (force-push, protected-branch push), not about the slice's natural mechanical conclusion. The wip-file's per-failure-mode amendment shapes are concrete enough to copy into the canonical skill bodies during a `drive-reconcile-skills` / `drive-update-skills` pass.

**Upstream candidate?** Yes — the orchestrator-using-operator-as-decision-validation-surface pattern is not specific to mongo-driver, prisma-next, or any harness; it generalises to every orchestrator-role drive-* workflow execution. The trust-gradient + recommendation-discipline + terseness-as-altitude-signal trio is the upstream-worthy material; the PR-open framing is also largely upstream-worthy (with minor harness-specific phrasing around the actual PR-creation invocation). The cross-cutting root cause — "the orchestrator was using the operator as a default decision-validation surface, even for decisions inside the orchestrator's intent-bearing authority" — is the load-bearing piece for the upstream PR's framing.

## 2026-05-27 · drive-close-project · win (mandatory final retro for mongo-driver-version-support)

Project closed at TML-2663 / PR #597 with all eight functional requirements satisfied (FR1–FR8) and all twelve project-DoD items met or explicitly skipped with rationale (see the close-out section of PR #597 for the per-item evidence table). Slice-level execution was clean — the implementer (composer-2.5-fast, dispatched in M-size discipline) landed the three runtime peer-dep moves, the two non-consumer declaration removals, the catalog bump to `mongodb ^7.2.0`, and the `collection.drop()` audit in three commits with no halt. The only spec deviation (`@prisma-next/target-mongo` retaining `devDependencies: { mongodb: catalog: }` for tests-only consumption) was surfaced for orchestrator decision rather than silently re-shaped, and folded back into the spec via an FR2 / PDoD8 amendment.

**What went well.** The recon-grounded design (`research/mongo-surface-area.md` + `research/open-questions.md`) retired every "what if Q resolves badly" branch *before* dispatch, keeping the slice firmly in M-sized territory. The single-major peer-dep policy was settled in `drive-discussion` early; the spec carried the model verbatim; no architectural re-litigation surfaced at execution time. The lockfile-coherence verification confirmed the predicted side-effect resolution (memory-server v11 already bundling driver-7 made the two-major coexistence vanish on bump).

**What surprised.** Recon's `src/`-only scan misclassified `@prisma-next/target-mongo` as a non-consumer (it imports `mongodb` in three integration tests). Structural-coherence check #3 in the slice plan was buggy on its own merits — line-oriented `rg` cannot match cross-section JSON structure. Both surfaced at implementation time — the most expensive surface. The implementer halted-and-surfaced both rather than silently improvising; the slice still landed cleanly but the falsification cost was real.

**Lessons landed where.** Five orchestrator-behavior failure modes captured at [`drive/calibration/failure-modes.md § F6`](../calibration/failure-modes.md#f6-orchestrator-over-asking-inside-an-approved-workflow-shape) (orchestrator over-asking inside an approved workflow shape) and [`§ F7`](../calibration/failure-modes.md#f7-orchestrator-wrong-altitude-response-to-terse-signalling-operator) (wrong-altitude response to terse-signalling operator). Three slice-execution learnings landed at [`§ F8`](../calibration/failure-modes.md#f8-recon-specialist-classifies-dependency-usage-by-src-only-scan) (recon `src/`-only scan), [`§ F9`](../calibration/failure-modes.md#f9-slice-plan-structural-coherence-checks-use-line-oriented-regex-on-structured-files) (structural-checks-need-jq), and the [Slice-shape scope traps](../calibration/failure-modes.md#slice-shape-scope-traps) list (dep-shape buckets vs blanket package.json statements). The version-bump-cycle methodology lives in the close-out section of PR #597 (no separate ADR — operator preference for ceremony minimisation; the methodology survives as part of the PR archive and the design-notes content folded inline).

**Deferred work.** Cursor `batchSize` explicit configuration (perf, deferred per spec open-question Q3 — v7 actually *reduces* round-trips, so a regression is unlikely). Pre-emptive `MongoClient.connect()` fail-fast hardening (deferred per Q4 — every site connects to credential-free memory-server). Neither has a follow-up Linear ticket; both gate on a future test / perf surprise that hasn't surfaced.

**ADR-worthy?** Yes — landed as [ADR 219 — Database driver coupling: framework owns user-facing types; driver is a single-major peer dependency](../../docs/architecture%20docs/adrs/ADR%20219%20-%20Database%20driver%20coupling%20-%20framework%20owns%20user-facing%20types%20and%20driver%20is%20a%20single-major%20peer%20dependency.md). Captures both the user-facing-type-surface ownership rule (framework re-exports from the driver; does not wrap with framework-owned identities) and the single-major peer-dependency rule, plus the major-bump audit cadence. Framed framework-wide so future `pg` / `sqlite` driver bumps reference the same policy. The initial close-out compromise — "fold the methodology into the PR body, no ADR" — was an instance of the F6 failure-mode (orchestrator defaulting against ceremony without checking whether the content actually generalises across extensions); the operator surfaced it post-close-out and the ADR landed in a follow-on commit.

**One-sentence summary.** Migrated `@prisma-next/mongo` and siblings from a pinned `mongodb@^6` to a single-supported-major peer-dep posture on `mongodb@^7` — users now control the install, Prisma Next still owns the user-facing type surface — in one PR with three implementer commits and zero scope thrash.

**Upstream candidate?** Yes for F6 / F7 (orchestrator-behavior, already routed). F8 / F9 / scope-trap bullet are mostly project-context (calibration-of-recon-briefs, calibration-of-validation-gate-scripts) but the underlying patterns ("recon-by-src-only", "regex-on-structured-files", "blanket-package.json-non-goals") generalise; mark as `maybe` for the synthesis ticket.
